nodes.reason: Single assertion: platform will not improve reasoning/debate.
Single claim with clarification (third-party focused, not debate-winning).
Cannot resolve + can map + Scott-vs-Reasonable is goal-disagreement (three independent claims).
Arguments lack clean structure (fact) + therefore maps assume false structure (inference).
Single claim about what matters in arguments: weighing, not premise resolution.
Example illustrating a single point about weighing in real arguments.
Single claim: values/frameworks cause disagreement and maps cannot resolve them.
Over-determination exists (fact) + therefore refuting premise doesn't move people (inference).
Mapping inefficient for minds + intended users barely exist + online arguers not seeking updates (three independent claims).
Unsolved for 2000 years (fact) + therefore unfixable by new tools (logical inference).
Single unified claim about altitude/level of decisive generators of disagreement.
Doesn't force premise-conclusion + uses specific features (graded edges, antithesis sets) + models over-determination (three independent propositions).
Single unified claim: goal is to locate/make weights explicit, shown by metrics.
Single claim about map behavior, with evidence from covid-origins graph.
Single unified claim about how Reasonable gives values first-class treatment.
Single unified claim: over-determination is native to Reasonable's grammar.
Wikipedia-like third-party artifact (claim 1) + cheap AI can build/maintain at scale (claim 2).
Single claim: AI agents demonstrated automation of map building is possible and cheap.
Single unified argument: changed conditions make 2000-year history irrelevant.
Single claim: Reasonable produces maps not consensus.
Consensus-vs-truth problem exists + needs external anchors + anchors are scarce (three independent claims).
Retired alignment + reputation inverts on bias + calibration must decline (three independent findings).
Dispersion is low-reliability signal (finding) + therefore contestedness must be read structurally (inference).
Single claim: adoption and behavior at scale with real humans is unproven.
Scott's strongest points are X/Y/Z + Reasonable concedes/addresses differently + therefore goal disagreement (three independent propositions).
Disagreement is over-determined and value-laden + won't be resolved by Scott's logic + Reasonable graphs handle it correctly.
Single evaluative claim: mapping-without-agreement is worth building.
Single evaluative claim: tool only worth building if it changes minds/produces agreement.
Single unified argument: arguments rarely hinge on false facts, so premise-finding tools are misdirected.
Single claim: AI-risk disagreement was about weighing, not facts/fallacies.
Single unified argument: dense premise maps worsen by misrepresenting probabilistic as necessary.
Single unified claim: Reasonable models jettison-and-continue, not premise-finding.

sets.reason: n002 (improves via mapping) and n003 (can map but depends on demands) can coexist; n003 adds nuance rather than contradicting.
n004 critiques traditional maps + n012 shows Reasonable addresses the critique; compatible, not contradictory.
n005 (weighing is key) and n013 (locate weighing) are compatible claims about same phenomenon.
n007 (can't resolve values) and n015 (maps values) coexist; n015 doesn't claim resolution, just representation.
n008 (over-determination exists) and n016 (Reasonable models it) are compatible descriptions.
n009 (inefficient for arguers) and n017 (use as third-party artifact instead) agree on problem and diverge on solution.
n010 asserts history proves unfixability; n019 asserts that proof is invalid due to changed conditions. Contradictory evidentiary claims.
n027 (mapping-without-agreement IS worth it) and n028 (is NOT worth it) are direct contradictions on value.
n029 (premise-finding is wrong) and n032 (Reasonable doesn't use it) are compatible; second shows solution to problem posed by first.
n031 (dense premise maps fail) and n012 (Reasonable uses graded support) are compatible; one describes problem, other shows solution.

nodes.reason: Bundles main claim (mapping cannot improve reasoning) + structural reason (misdescription) + practical reason (not usable) + inference (endeavor is doomed)
Bundles: platform succeeds + by faithful representation + for different audience + thereby meets objections
Bundles: success hinges on goal definition + dispute is value-laden + can only be mapped not settled
Single claim: forcing into logical map misrepresents probabilistic weighing
Bundles: true generators are weighing/values + positions are over-determined + no single element moves them
Two independent claims: formal mapping is inefficient AND user barely exists
Bundles: history hasn't solved debate + multiple platform limitations + conclusion all point same direction
Bundles: design features model weighing (empirical) + outcome is normal not failure (evaluative)
Bundles: goal is to make weighing explicit + values as first-class + unresolved counts as success
Two separable claims: intended audience is third-party AND low-cost automation removes cost barrier
Bundles: non-solution reflects absent conditions + these conditions are genuinely new + parallels other tools
Meta-claim bundling: critique targets resolution + conceded + defense targets representation + argument talks past itself
Bundles: success is over-determined/value-laden + therefore cannot be settled by argument + only located
Bundles: inability to resolve is distinct from inability to represent (empirical) + conceding former doesn't concede latter (logical)
Value judgment with conditions: worth for faithful mapping + making value-cruxes explicit + even without agreement
Bundles: worth only if changes minds/produces agreement (value claim) + because leaving unchanged isn't worth it (reason)
Single claim about what load-bearing work consists of
Single claim: disagreements generated by differing values not checkable fact
Single claim about how positions are held: many weak vs single decisive
Single claim: disagreements rarely decompose into form maps assume
Single claim: people abandon fact but continue position on other grounds
Single claim: some disagreements trace entirely to different weighing
Evidence example illustrating single point
Single claim with necessary consequence about misrepresentation
Evidence demonstrating complexity of reaching verdict
Examples illustrating how values drive disagreement
Reason and consequence tightly bound: because held through many, changing one typically fails
Single claim: formal mapping is inefficient compared to ordinary argument
Single claim about imagined user barely existing
Single claim: online arguers typically not motivated to update beliefs
Design feature with necessary consequence: graded edges enable partial backing
Design principle: rival positive representation means links not binary
Design feature: joint-support groups weak considerations mirroring reality
Design feature: re-wording stacks preserve support despite wording shifts
Single mechanism: graded map reduces support but doesn't delete claim
Bundles: historical fact (philosophy hasn't solved) + inference (therefore not just tooling gap)
Bundles: definition of success (if=agreement) + builders concede they don't achieve it
Single claim: consensus-vs-truth problem remains unresolved
Mechanism and consequence tightly bound: rules measure crowd, so inverted on biased crowd
Two independent factual claims: requires external anchors AND anchors scarce on contested questions
Fact and consequence tightly bound: dispersion unreliable so must infer structurally
Single claim: adoption at scale by real humans remains unproven
Evidence: demonstrations used AI agents not humans
Single claim about success definition for contested questions
Bundles design features (values get dedicated type + is/ought firewall) with consequence
Evidence example: unresolved verdict scored well
Design rule with tight reason: rated by endorsement because values aren't truth-apt
Single claim about intended audience
Criticism and reason tightly bound: efficiency criterion wrong because not built to persuade them
Single claim: AI authorship removes cost that doomed earlier efforts
Evidence: three questions built by AI agents
Single claim: automated authorship/rating is genuinely new capability
Single claim: reputation-weighting is mechanism earlier efforts lacked
Single claim: blind rating before consensus is unavailable mechanism
Single claim: tools languished until medium arrived
Evidence: three questions built demonstrating low-cost authorship at scale
Single claim with necessary consequence: success depends on multiple weak considerations
Single claim: success determination hinges on value judgment not checkable fact
Descriptive claim listing two positions; single phenomenon
Single claim: over-determined disputes can locate but not resolve
Single factual claim: defense concedes tool doesn't make disagreers agree
Single claim: strongest objections concern resolution-achievement
Single claim: defense makes representation claims not resolution claims
Evidence: unresolved verdict scored well
Single claim: strongest objections target resolution not faithful representation
Bundles: definition of success (if=agreement) + builders concede they don't achieve it
Design principle with necessary consequence: dedicated type means values represented-as-values
Design rule with tight reason: rated by endorsement because values not truth-apt

sets.reason: Members n002 (platform succeeds by representing) and n003 (whether it works depends on goal definition) can both be true simultaneously; n003 is compatible with n002's answer to the definitional question
n004 claims forcing into logical map misrepresents weighing; n008 claims graded edges, rivals, groupings DO model weighing properly. These directly contradict about whether the map form succeeds

Members n005 (weighing operates beneath surface claims) and n009 (goal is to make weighing explicit) are complementary and both true simultaneously: one describes the problem, one describes the solution

n007 concludes historical non-solution and current issues point toward doom/unsolvability; n011 interprets history as reflecting absent conditions (not unsolvability) and asserts new conditions now exist, reaching opposite conclusion about solvability

n015 claims tool is worth building for representation even without agreement; n016 claims worth only if produces agreement. These are contrary value judgments: worth for X-without-Y vs worth only-if-Y

nodes.reason: Single claim: platforms will not improve reasoning/debate
Single claim with qualifier: maps improve reasoning as third-party artifact, not debate-winning tool
"Cannot RESOLVE disagreement" + "can MAP it" + "dispute is about GOAL not mechanism" - three distinct propositions
Single claim: map form presumes logical skeleton that real disagreement lacks
Single claim about how real arguments are structured
"Rarely hinge on false fact/fallacy" AND "when pointed out people drop it and argue elsewhere"
Evidence node illustrating one point: experts disagreed on weighting, not facts
"Adding premises makes maps worse" AND "this recasts probabilistic reasoning as logical"
Single claim: maps settle premises but that is not where disagreements are decided
Single claim: weighting magnitudes is the load-bearing work
Evidence node: even with fact true, complex weighing is required
Single claim: deepest disagreements come from values
Single claim: positions held through many weakly-held considerations
Single claim: refuting single consideration rarely changes position
"Decisions happen at high-level worldview" AND "maps operate at surface level (wrong)"
Single claim: argument-mapping not practically viable for debate
Single claim: formal mapping is inefficient for changing minds
Single claim with elaboration: people do not want to argue rigorously
Single inferential claim: unsolved history implies unfixable
Single claim: conceded limitations count against platforms working
Single conditional claim: if working=agreement, maps do not work
"Rating rules measure crowd agreement only" AND "on bias they reward bias"
Single claim: correction needs external truth, scarce on contested questions
Three empirical findings: reputation rule buried raters; discrimination inverted; calibration declined
Single claim: dispersion is low-reliability signal, structure is better
Single claim with evidence: adoption by humans is unproven
Single claim: maps do not need logical form, graded grammar works better
Three design choices: "graded not binary" + "positive rivals not negation" + "joint grounds"
Single claim: graphs handle over-determined positions natively
Single claim: how graded maps handle refutation as normal case
Single claim: proper job is to locate weighing/values, not resolve
Single claim: goal is to locate weighting, not resolve it
Evidence node: honest "unresolved" verdict scored high
Single claim: value claims can be represented separately
Single claim: maps have viable audience and production route
Single claim: audience is third parties, not live arguers
Single claim: AI agents can build/rate maps at scale
Evidence node: three questions successfully mapped by AI
Single claim: inference from history fails due to new conditions
Single claim: history does not imply unsolvable with new conditions
Single claim: longstanding failure before success is normal for tools
Single claim: two sides argue about purpose, not mechanism
Single claim: meta-dispute is contested and value-laden
Single evaluative claim: such a tool is worth building
Single evaluative claim: only worth building if changes minds

sets.reason: n001 (will NOT improve) and n002 (WILL improve for third parties) directly contradict on whether meaningful improvement occurs
n004 says maps MUST assume logical form; n027 says they do NOT have to. Direct contradiction on form requirements
n009 describes limitation (maps settle premises); n031 describes proper job (locate weighing). Compatible: descriptive vs prescriptive
n016 (not viable for debate) and n035 (viable for third parties) refer to different purposes. Both can hold
n019 (history unsolved implies unfixable) and n040 (does NOT imply unfixable) directly contradict on logical inference
n008 identifies problem (premises make worse); n028 proposes solution (alternatives exist). Compatible: problem and solution
n044 (tool IS worth building) and n045 (only IF changes minds) directly contradict on evaluative worth
